text: Robotic transabdominal preperitoneal epigastric hernia repair. Our patient is a fifty nine year old female with a painful and enlarging recurrent epigastric slash incisional hernia. She complained of pain at the site and progressive bulging. Past medical history is significant for diabetes, hypertension and obesity with a BMI of thirty one. Her past surgical history is significant for an open cholecystectomy in the remote past and an open incisional hernia repair with placement of an intraperitoneal mesh. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, which reveals a small epigastric recurrence containing fat. She also has a small umbilical hernia.

A 12mm trocar is inserted through the umbilical hernia defect, followed by the placement of 8mm trocars spaced 10 centimeters apart. The camera is positioned at least 15 centimeters away from the inferior aspect of the defect to facilitate incision of the peritoneum for adequate mesh overlap. The robotic system is docked over the patient's shoulder.
The surgeon uses three instruments: a ProGrasp, monopolar scissors, and a suture cut needle driver to perform lysis of adhesions, reduce the hernia content, and clear the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes that the colon is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, noting the need to minimize contact with the bowel using the ProGrasp instrument due to its strong grip strength. If grasping the bowel becomes necessary, a fenestrated bipolar instrument is preferred.
The surgeon identifies old mesh from a previous operation and decides to explant it, which creates a visible peritoneal defect that may affect the pre-peritoneal hernia repair approach.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision at least five centimeters below the inferior aspect of the recurrent hernia defect. A preperitoneal flap is developed, and the surgeon carefully stays within the preperitoneal plane while working around the central defect caused by the mesh explantation.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum to ensure sufficient coverage for re-peritonealization of an uncoated mesh. The partner palpates the subxiphoid plane to maintain orientation, revealing a large preperitoneal pocket.

The surgeon sizes the hernia defect, measuring three by six centimeters, using surgical instruments. An OV-lock absorbable suture on a CT-I equivalent needle is prepared, with the needles skeeted to fit through an eight millimeter trocar.

The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury during the closure of the primary defect and the placement of a fifteen by fifteen centimeter sheet of proline soft mesh into the preperitoneal space. A secure strap is used to attach the mesh to the abdominal wall, avoiding high placement where counter pressure is not feasible. The mesh is then re-peritonealized, fitting into place like a jigsaw puzzle.
